title: Autonomous vehicle fatally strikes pedestrian during public road testing programme
date_note: Date of the fatal incident as recorded in NTSB Accident Report HWY18MH010.
ai_system_context: Autonomous vehicle perception and control system operating in test mode on a public road. The vehicle was in autonomous mode with a safety driver present at the time of the incident.
summary: According to NTSB Accident Report HWY18MH010, published in November 2019, an autonomous test vehicle struck and fatally injured a pedestrian who was crossing a public road outside a designated crosswalk in Tempe, Arizona, on 18 March 2018. The NTSB investigation found that the vehicle's AI perception system detected an object in the road but repeatedly reclassified it, ultimately failing to initiate emergency braking in time to avoid the collision. The report further found that the safety driver monitoring system had been disabled, and that the safety driver was not attentive at the time of the incident. The NTSB identified the operating company's inadequate safety culture and the failure to implement adequate safety risk assessment procedures as contributing factors.
evidence_required: EV-005 — Testing record: evidence of pre-deployment edge-case testing for pedestrian detection in low-light and off-crosswalk scenarios
EV-004 — Human oversight record: evidence of safety driver monitoring procedures and their operational status
EV-002 — Risk assessment: documented safety case for public road autonomous vehicle testing programmes
EV-014 — Post-incident review: NTSB final report and organisational post-incident review records
lessons: Autonomous vehicle testing programmes operating on public roads require robust, independently verified safety cases that account for edge-case perception failures before deployment.
Safety driver monitoring systems must remain operational and enforced throughout autonomous vehicle public road testing; their disablement should require explicit safety justification.

Pre-deployment testing for autonomous vehicle AI systems should include systematic evaluation of object classification reliability under varied environmental conditions, including low-light and off-crosswalk pedestrian scenarios.
Organisations conducting autonomous vehicle testing should maintain an active safety risk assessment process that is reviewed before each public road testing phase.

sources.title: NTSB Accident Report HWY18MH010 — Collision Between Vehicle Controlled by Developmental Automated Driving System and Pedestrian, Tempe, Arizona, March 18, 2018

affected_stakeholders: Pedestrians in autonomous vehicle public road testing zones
Regulators overseeing autonomous vehicle testing on public roads
impact: A pedestrian fatality resulted. The NTSB investigation contributed to increased regulatory scrutiny of autonomous vehicle testing safety practices and prompted updates to safety guidance for testing programmes. The operating company suspended its public road autonomous testing programme following the incident.
